title: 6 Movie Costumes That Didn't Just Get Worn — They Got Sold Out
dek: From a 2,000% sales spike to a sweatshirt that was literally a laundry accident, these are the on-screen looks that walked straight off the reel and into everyone's closet.

items.heading: Tom Cruise Wore These Sunglasses In One Movie And Basically Saved A Company
items.text: Before Risky Business, Ray-Ban was quietly circling the drain — the Wayfarer had fallen so out of favor that the brand was about to kill the style entirely. Then Tom Cruise put them on for the 1983 teen comedy, and everything changed overnight.

images.caption: Tom Cruise, the man who single-handedly kept Ray-Ban in business
Risky Business (1983) — the movie that made Wayfarers a phenomenon

items.heading: Three Years Later, Cruise Did It Again — This Time With Aviators
items.text: Just when Ray-Ban thought lightning couldn't strike twice, Top Gun happened. Costume designer Marlene Stewart worked directly with the props department to custom-fit Cruise's Aviators for maximum swagger, and the results speak for themselves.

images.caption: Top Gun (1986) sent Aviator sales soaring in months
As Maverick, Cruise made military-issue shades a wardrobe staple

items.heading: That Iconic Off-The-Shoulder Sweatshirt? Total Laundry Accident
items.text: Jennifer Beals' legendary Flashdance look wasn't designed at all — it started when her own sweatshirt shrank in the dryer and she cut a hole in the neck just to get it back on. She wore it to a fitting, costume designer Michael Kaplan and director Adrian Lyne fell in love with it on sight, and a defining '80s silhouette was born by pure accident.

images.caption: Flashdance (1983) turned a shrunk sweatshirt into a fashion movement
Jennifer Beals, whose ruined laundry became an '80s icon

items.heading: Diane Keaton Basically Dressed Herself — And Started A Menswear Craze
items.text: Costume designer Ruth Morley reportedly hated Annie Hall's mixed-up ties, vests and khakis, but Woody Allen overruled her and let Keaton wear her own eclectic wardrobe. Ralph Lauren contributed a few pieces too, and the tomboyish look went on to define late-'70s androgynous fashion — with Lauren revisiting the tweed-and-trouser aesthetic in his own runway collections decades later.

images.caption: Diane Keaton, whose closet became a fashion movement
Annie Hall (1977) made ties and vests a women's wardrobe staple

items.heading: That Yellow Plaid Set Almost Wasn't Yellow At All
items.text: Costume designer Mona May originally tried Cher's iconic first-day-of-school suit in blue, then red, before finally landing on the bright yellow Jean Paul Gaultier set that made the entire cast gasp in the fitting room. Women's Wear Daily crowned Clueless "the fashion movie of the year" in 1995, and nearly 30 years later designers from Versace to Dior are still riffing on "Cher plaid."

images.caption: Clueless (1995) — the plaid set that's still being copied on runways
Alicia Silverstone as Cher, in the yellow suit that almost didn't happen

items.heading: Grease Made The Leather Jacket A Uniform For Two Different Tribes At Once
items.text: Costume designer Albert Wolsky leaned hard into 1950s greaser style for the T-Birds' jackets, while Sandy's own leather-and-spandex transformation at the end of the film became just as iconic — the actual jacket and pants later sold at auction for over $400,000. The look has been reinterpreted for every Grease revival since, including the Paramount+ prequel series decades later.

images.caption: Grease (1978) — Sandy's finale jacket sold at auction for over $400K
John Travolta as Danny Zuko, greaser-jacket icon
